design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate castle rooms by pushing/positioning objects (blocks, barrels, candles, elevators) to unlock doors and progress toward the princess, while avoiding enemies
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Puzzle-solving under time pressure with enemy avoidance; tension between methodical object placement and real-time hazards
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Rescue Princess Margarita from Groken Castle through clever puzzle-solving and navigation rather than direct combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects action-oriented dungeon crawling in favor of environmental puzzle logic; emphasizes precision timing and spatial reasoning over reflexes; early Metroidvania design with grid-based room progression and map-based exploration

difficulty.lives_system: NES version starts with 50 lives (vs. Japanese Famicom version with 4 lives); generous in quantity but each death represents failure to execute precise room solution
difficulty.continues_system: No continue system; must complete game in single sitting or restart stage via controller 2 menu (NES version lacks save feature present in Japanese Famicom)
difficulty.checkpointing: Room-level reset via controller 2 menu; no mid-room checkpoints; map system shows princess location to guide progression
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Low; fixed puzzle layouts with single correct solution per room; no difficulty selection; challenge comes from understanding object physics and door-opening mechanics rather than adjustable parameters

progression.upgrade_system: Key collection (color-matched keys required for specific doors); weapon acquisition (sword/dagger available in NES/Famicom versions for close-range enemy elimination)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression locked behind spatial puzzles requiring object alignment; map acquisition reveals 10x10 room grid and princess location; keys gate access to new areas
replayability_hooks: Comparing different object-positioning strategies across rooms
NES version challenge: completing without save feature in single session

world_building_techniques: Castle architecture and room layout convey dungeon progression
Enemy types (Knights, Bishops, Wizards, Fire Spirits, Vikings, Phantom Flowers) establish thematic hazard variety
Princess location marker on map drives player motivation and exploration direction
Title/objective (rescue Princess Margarita) frames all puzzle-solving as purposeful rescue mission

danger_signals: Enemy types escalate in thematic complexity (Knights → Bishops → Wizards → supernatural entities like Fire Spirits and Phantom Flowers)
Environmental hazards increase: escalators used to trap/crush enemies suggest progressive danger
environmental_storytelling: Castle room variety (10x10 grid suggests vast, multi-floor structure)
Object types in rooms (barrels, candles, blocks, elevators) suggest functional castle spaces being repurposed for puzzles
Map display format implies cartographic/strategic exploration mindset

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Castlequest NES page)
